title: The Second Sunday of Advent
context:
# TEMPORAL CONTEXT SNAPSHOT
Saint John's Episcopal Cathedral, Denver, Colorado
Sermon Date: December 6, 2020

## 1. LITURGICAL CONTEXT

This sermon was preached on the Second Sunday of Advent in Year A of the liturgical calendar. The Revised Common Lectionary readings for this date would have included Isaiah 11:1-10 (the peaceable kingdom), Psalm 72:1-7, 18-19, Romans 15:4-13 (hope and encouragement), and Matthew 3:1-12 (John the Baptist's call to repentance). Advent, the four-week season of preparation before Christmas, typically emphasizes themes of hope, repentance, and anticipation of Christ's coming, with particular focus on John the Baptist in the second week.

## 2. EPISCOPAL / ANGLICAN CHURCH NEWS

The Episcopal Church was navigating significant denominational divisions in late 2020, particularly around sexuality and theological interpretation, though no major General Convention actions were occurring at this specific time (the triennial General Convention having met in July 2018). The Diocese of Colorado under Bishop Robert Wright was engaged in ongoing pandemic-related ministry adjustments, with many congregations still operating under COVID-19 restrictions and hybrid worship formats. Saint John's Cathedral itself, as the seat of the diocese, would have been observing the denomination's guidance on limited in-person worship capacity during the pandemic surge of December 2020.

## 3. DENVER & COLORADO NEWS

Colorado was in the midst of a severe COVID-19 surge in early December 2020, with hospitals reaching capacity constraints and Governor Jared Polis issuing increasingly strict public health orders on November 20-25. Denver saw rising case counts and hospitalizations that week, prompting concerns about healthcare system overwhelm throughout the Front Range. The weather in Denver during early December 2020 was unseasonably warm and dry, a notable contrast to typical winter conditions.

## 4. NATIONAL NEWS (US)

The nation was focused on the final stages of the 2020 presidential election aftermath, with President Trump's legal challenges to the November 3 results ongoing and recounts underway in Georgia and Wisconsin, even as electoral certification deadlines approached (December 8 was the Safe Harbor deadline). COVID-19 case counts were surging nationally with over 200,000 new cases reported daily by early December, and the first vaccine doses (Pfizer/BioNTech) had just received emergency use authorization on December 2, with distribution beginning within days. The Supreme Court had rejected Trump's Texas lawsuit seeking to overturn the election results on December 3, just days before this sermon.

## 5. INTERNATIONAL NEWS

The United Kingdom was finalizing its post-Brexit trade negotiations with the European Union, with a deal framework reached on December 3, 2020, potentially avoiding economic disruption at year-end. The global death toll from COVID-19 had reached approximately 1.5 million by early December 2020, with European nations implementing strict lockdowns as case counts surged. Iran-US tensions remained elevated with the aftermath of Qasem Soleimani's killing a year prior still shaping regional dynamics in Iraq and the Persian Gulf.

## 6. CULTURAL MOMENT

December 2020 carried a peculiar mix of holiday anticipation shadowed by profound pandemic fatigue and election-related anxiety; many Americans were contemplating their second consecutive holiday season disrupted by COVID-19 restrictions. The zeitgeist was marked by exhaustion from nearly nine months of pandemic life, uncertainty about the incoming Biden administration, and the emotional toll of restricted gatherings during a season traditionally centered on family and community. Culturally, streaming services and holiday entertainment were major focal points, while COVID-related disruptions dominated professional sports and entertainment; the NFL and other leagues were managing outbreaks while attempting to complete seasons, and Broadway remained dark.
